Question: The buyer of a commercial property under a sale/leaseback arrangement would be least concerned with:
A: the depreciated book value of the building.
B: the credit rating of the seller.
C: the location of the property in the general community.
Feedback / Explanation: In a sale-leaseback transaction, the seller becomes a tenant of the new buyer, which allows the seller to deduct all of his future rent payments as business expenses. In these types of arrangements, the buyer is generally not concerned with the book value- instead, they are concerned with the income generated by the business of the seller.

Question: Which of the following would not be subject to property tax?
B: Possessory interests of lessees in tax-exempt public property, such as leases on oil and gas properties
C: Vacant land located in an unincorporated area of the county
D: Mobile homes properly installed on a permanent foundation
Feedback / Explanation: Intangible personal property is not subject to taxation. Real property and tangible personal property are subject to taxation.

Question: The seller conveyed a quitclaim deed to the buyer. Upon receipt of the deed, the buyer may be certain that:
A: The buyer now owns the property subject to certain claims of the seller.
C: There are no encumbrances against the property.
D: All of the seller's interests in the property belong to the buyer.
Feedback / Explanation: It's important to remember that quit claim deeds have no guarantees beyond the signer's declaration that he or she is giving up all claims to the title. Accordingly, they are generally used to clear up minor issues and almost never as the primary form of transferring ownership.

Question: Which of the following is a means of transferring title from one party to another?
Feedback / Explanation: Deeds are the written form by which land is transferred from one owner to another. In order to be legal, they must be executed, delivered by the current owner and accepted by the new owner.

Question: When a company furnishes materials for the construction of a house and is subsequently not paid, it may file a(n):
Feedback / Explanation: A mechanic's lien is the first, and usually most cost-effective, step for a person providing labor and/or materials to a homeowner to recover monies owed--in large part because of the pressure it puts on the homeowner to settle quickly and without costly court involvement.

Question: What is the opposite of "alienation?"
Feedback / Explanation: Alienation of title is the conveyance of title, so the opposite is the acquisition of title.

Question: Recorded title to a parcel of real property was vested in Jill Martinez, a single woman. After her marriage to Josh Cohen, she executed a deed to the property only in the name of Jill Cohen, a married woman. The discrepancy in the grantor's name is:
A: not important as long as the property is adequately described.
B: a defect which could cause separate property of both spouses to become joint tenancy property.
C: cured after the deed is on freehold for one year.
D: a defect which may cause a cloud on the title.
Feedback / Explanation: A "cloud on title" means there is something affecting the title that may prevent its transfer. For example, if a single woman gets married and she purchased real property prior to marriage, then executed the grant deed in her married name, a discrepancy would arise regarding the grantor's name. This might constitute enough of a defect to result in a cloud on the title.

Question: Title to real property can be acquired by an individual by all of the following methods except:
Feedback / Explanation: Escheat is a reversion of property to the state in the absence of an individual owner. It usually occurs when a property owner dies intestate (without a will), and without heirs. An individual CANNOT acquire property through Escheat.

Question: "Intestate succession" occurs when:
A: Not all heirs are named in a formal will.
B: The deceased leaves only a holographic will.
Feedback / Explanation: Intestate succession occurs when someone dies without leaving a will, or leaving an invalid will so that the property of the estate passes by the laws of succession rather than by the direction of the deceased.

Question: Which of the following deeds would least likely contain implied covenants by the grantor?
Feedback / Explanation: A Quitclaim Deed is a deed operating as a release; intended to pass any title, interest, or claim which the grantor may have in the property, but not containing any warranty of a valid interest or title in the grantor.

Question: Which of the following is not essential to a valid deed?
C: It must be acknowledged by a notary or public official
Feedback / Explanation: Deeds provide evidence of the transfer of title of real property from a grantor to a grantee. The deed requires the grantor to be legally competent; it must be delivered and accepted, and it contains a granting or action clause. In most states, a deed does NOT need to be acknowledged by a notary or public official to be valid.

Question: A lis pendens:
A: only affects title to real property if the owner is not a party to the lawsuit.
B: can only be removed from the public records by a court order.
C: can be recorded no matter what the type of lawsuit is.
D: may affect title to real property based on the results of the lawsuit.
Feedback / Explanation: A "lis pendens" is a legal document which gives notice that an action or legal proceeding (mechanic's lien filing, foreclosure) is pending in the courts that could affect the title to the designated property.

Question: An order from a court to a designated official to sell property in order to pay a judgment is called:
Feedback / Explanation: A court order instructing an officer of the court to carry out the decision of the court, such as the selling of foreclosed property or the removal of a tenant at sufferance.

Question: In making the decision of whether or not to issue a title policy, the title insurance company would be most concerned with those documents that appear within the:
Feedback / Explanation: The chain of title is the history of all of the documents that transfer title to a parcel of real property, starting with the earliest existing document and ending with the most recent. Note that the chain of title only tracks documents that transfer title to a property.

Question: A title insurance company official would have known that an "abstract of title” is a:
A: Standard form that is used in the industry
B: Closing statement from the title insurance company
D: Written summary of documents relating to the title of the property
Feedback / Explanation: An abstract of title is a summary of the history of the ownership of a property, starting with the original grant, and including all subsequent encumbrances and conveyances.

Question: A buyer would want which of the following removed from a preliminary title report?
B: An appurtenant easement over adjacent property.
Feedback / Explanation: A preliminary title report is issued early in the transaction to reveal all matters that presently affect the title, such as liens or judgments on the property or easements and covenants. A buyer would not be pleased to see a mechanic's lien on the report and would most likely want that taken care of.

Question: A standard title insurance policy insures against:
B: A recorded deed in the chain of title that was not properly delivered
C: Losses sustained by improved property only
D: Claims of persons in possession of the property
Feedback / Explanation: Standard policy of title insurance protects against forgery and improper delivery, however, it does not include a survey or on-site inspection.

Question: There are several differences between an extended coverage policy of title insurance and a standard coverage policy of title insurance. Which of the following is insured under the extended policy, but not under the standard policy?
B: The results of a forged deed in the chain title
C: The lack of capacity of one of the parties to any transaction involving title to the land
D: The possibility that some improvements on the insured property are located on adjoining land
Feedback / Explanation: Extended policy covers improvements on adjoining land.

Question: Although a title insurance policy does cover the risk of loss for many reasons, which of the following risks is not covered in a title insurance policy?
A: An unpaid city tax not shown in the policy
B: A change in zoning ordinance, regulation or plan
C: A document in the series of records is forged
D: Failure of husband or wife to sign the deed
Feedback / Explanation: Title insurance generally doesn't cover changes in zoning or city regulations.

Question: Which type of policy will cover everything?
Feedback / Explanation: Title insurance policy is a policy that protects a buyer against errors or omissions or defects in the title of the property. No title insurance policy covers everything.

Question: A title company could make a title search by searching the records of the:
A: County Recorder's, Federal Land and County Clerk's office
Feedback / Explanation: A title search is a review of all recorded documents affecting a specific piece of property to determine the present condition of title.

Question: An agreement wherein one party agrees to reimburse the other party for damages suffered in the event of a clearly defined risk, in exchange for payment of monetary consideration, is commonly known as:
Feedback / Explanation: Insurance is the promise of reimbursement for damages for the promise to pay. With respect to Fire Insurance, the policy should allow the insured to neither gain nor lose in the event of claim.

Question: The IRS (Internal Revenue Service) would define marginal tax rate as:
A: The tax rate which is used for the next dollar of taxable income
B: The difference between the present tax rate and the past tax rate
C: None of the other options are correct
D: The tax rate used for your state income taxes
Feedback / Explanation: Marginal Tax Rate is the tax rate that is applied to the next dollar of taxable earned income.

Question: A written instrument which passes title of property, such as furniture purchased as a part of a home sale, from seller to buyer would be a:
Feedback / Explanation: Bills of Sale are written instruments which passes title of personal property, such as furniture purchased as a part of a home sale, from seller to buyer. They are not used for real property. A Bill of Sale transfers title to personal property in much the same way that a deed transfers title to real property.

Question: An apartment house was purchased for $180,000. At the time of purchase, it had a fair market value of $200,000, and an assessed value of $140,000. The basis of the property for federal income tax purposes would be:
Feedback / Explanation: Basis is the value placed upon property for purposes of capital gains and depreciation. The basis upon purchase of real property for investment purposes is generally the cost referred to as the original cost basis, so in the case the purchase price of $180,000 is the property's basis for tax purposes.

Question: Which of the following would alienate title to property:
D: Securing an ALTA policy of title insurance
Feedback / Explanation: Conveying the title will alienate (transfer from one owner to another) the title to property.

Question: What item will appear as a debit on the buyer's closing statement?
C: Homeowner's insurance that had been prepaid by the seller
D: None of the other options are correct
Feedback / Explanation: The price is always a credit for the seller and a debit for the buyer. NOTE: Any seller-prepaid homeowners insurance will show up as a credit to the seller only. If the buyer's purchase is financed, a new homeowner's policy would need to be written and obtained by the buyer and could show up in the closing statement as homeowner's insurance prepaid by the buyer.

Question: Larry owned an income property with an adjusted cost basis of $150,000 and a fair market value of $200,000. He exchanged the property for another income property, which has a fair market value of $210,000. Both properties had no loans against them and no adjustment was made for the difference in value. For federal income tax purposes, the new property will have a basis for Larry of:
Feedback / Explanation: In a tax deferred exchange transaction, the cost basis of the property transferred becomes the cost basis of the property which is acquired or received.

Question: One who is authorized to perform certain acts for another under a power of attorney would be a:
A: None of the other options are correct
Feedback / Explanation: A type of agency relationship where one person holds a POWER OF ATTORNEY allowing him to execute legal documents on behalf of another. Decisions made by the attorney in fact are binding on the principal.

Question: Which of the following terms BEST describes personal property that, by its attachment to real property, is regarded as real estate?
Feedback / Explanation: Fixtures are objects attached to real property or incorporated into the land which are now considered real property. In determining whether or not an item of Personal Property has become a Fixture, use the Maria test (Method Adaptability Relationship Intention Agreement). The size, cost and time of attachment are not factors. Examples of fixtures would be garage doors, a pool covering or a chandelier.

Question: A title insurance policy will protect a buyer from financial losses that are caused by:
A: an encumbrance to which the policy has taken an exception.
B: a recorded easement to which the policy has taken an exception.
D: an unrecorded easement to which the policy has not taken an exception.
Feedback / Explanation: Title insurance is provided to ensure that the buyer is obtaining clean and marketable title. A title insurance policy insures against losses due to defects or problems not identified by the title search or examination, such as an unrecorded easement for which no exception was taken because it was not discovered during the title search. Allowable exceptions to title, including any easements or liens against the property that are repaid in full when the property is sold (such as a current mortgage) are generally not covered by the title insurance policy, but allow the policy to be issued. Title exceptions that are not permitted, like ongoing property disputes or outstanding tax liens, usually must be resolved before the title insurance will be issued.

Question: When private property is abandoned, the state may acquire title to that property under the right of:
Feedback / Explanation: Escheat is a reversion of property to the state in the absence of an individual owner. Usually occurs when a property owner dies intestate (without a will), and without heirs. An individual CANNOT acquire property through Escheat.

Question: Which of the following documents is usually prepared when a real estate buyer also purchases a seller's riding lawn mower?
Feedback / Explanation: A bill of sale is a written document that serves as evidence of the transfer of title to personal property. Personal property is generally movable property, an example of a bill of sale would be a lawn mower.

Question: The donation of private land for public use is called:
Feedback / Explanation: Dedication is the application of privately-owned land to the public for no consideration, with the intent that the land will be accepted and used for public purposes. A landowner may dedicate the entire fee simple interest, or an easement such as a public right-of-way across his property. Lands thus dedicated are normally taxed at a preferential rate.

Question: A mechanic's lien would be properly classified as a(n):
Feedback / Explanation: Mechanic's lien is a specific lien a lien affecting or attaching only to a certain, specific parcel of land or piece of property. A mechanic's lien is a security interest in the title to property for the benefit of those who have supplied labor or materials that improve the property.

Question: Under which of the following types of liens can both the real property and the personal property of the debtor be sold to pay the debt?
Feedback / Explanation: Most liens are against a specific property, such as a primary residence. Thus, a contractor seeking payment for a new deck cannot have a homeowner's car attached in settlement. A judgment lien, however, is a decision directed by the courts and can apply to whatever assets it deems appropriate.

Question: The covenant in a deed which states that the grantor is the owner and has the right to convey the title is called:
Feedback / Explanation: Another outgrowth of the feudal system, "seisin" derives from the French meaning to "sit upon or own" and gives owners the right to sell or transfer property at will.

Question: Which of the following instruments proves a marketable title?
Feedback / Explanation: A general warranty deed stipulates that the ownership being conveyed is free of any hidden liens, encumbrances or other claims. Further, it states that if some unknown encumbrance should arise in the future, the person conveying the deed will make appropriate compensation. (This guarantee, however, is no substitute for title insurance.)

Question: The title to real estate passes when a valid deed is:
Feedback / Explanation: Fundamentally, real estate transactions only involve two parties--the buyer and the seller. All that's necessary to create a legal sale is for one party to make an offer the other accepts. Recording, escrow, real estate licensees, mortgage companies and the like facilitate and support the transaction process but are not requirements of a legal sale.

Question: A special warranty deed differs from a general warranty deed in that the grantor's covenant in the special warranty deed:
A: protects all subsequent owners of the property.
B: applies only to a definite limited time.
C: is implied and is not written in full.
D: covers the time back to the original title.
Feedback / Explanation: Under a special warranty deed, an owner transfers property guaranteeing the quality of title only during the period of his or her ownership, leaving subsequent buyers vulnerable to prior claims. The more common deed in most states is the general warranty, because it establishes the ownership trail and validity of title going back to the original recorded ownership (for example, the purchase of Manhattan Island and all subsequent divisions, subdivisions and resales).

Question: A partition suit is used for which of the following?
A: to force a division of property without all the owners' consent
C: to allow construction of party walls
D: to change a tenancy by entireties to some other form of ownership
Feedback / Explanation: Partition suits are typically pursued when a co-owner of a property wants to sell their share and the other owners are opposed. Since it is a legal action involving the courts, it is an expense with often unsatisfactory results.

Question: A summary of the recorded history of matters that affect the title to a specific parcel of real property is called a(n):
Feedback / Explanation: Abstracts give an at-a-glance history of all the recorded documents associated with a property, including changes in title, liens, mortgages taken out and paid off, transfers through death and so forth. Chain of title is a history of ownership only.

Question: Which of the following is an example of involuntary alienation?
A: letting another person plant crops on an unused portion of a piece of land
B: giving a piece of land to the zoo
C: having a piece of land sold for delinquent taxes
D: selling a property to pay off debts
Feedback / Explanation: In legal terms "alienation" means transferring, which can be voluntary--as in the case of a sale--or "involuntary," which means the property is taken from the owner without his consent. In addition to enforcing liens, involuntary alienation can also occur through eminent domain, adverse possession and other circumstances.

Question: When an owner sues the government to get them to pay for taking his property, this is known as:
Feedback / Explanation: Inverse condemnation is a term used in the law to describe a situation in which the government takes private property but fails to pay the just compensation required by the Constitution. In order to be compensated, the owner must then sue the government. In such cases the owner is the plaintiff and that is why the action is called inverse. The order of parties is reversed, as compared to direct condemnation where the government is the plaintiff who sues a defendant-owner to take his property.

Question: A tax bill derived by calculating the cost-per-front-foot, is a:
Feedback / Explanation: Special assessments are generally for repayment to the government for the cost of installing utilities, or curbs and gutters. Each owner is billed their share of the expense calculated by their proportion of the benefit to their property. The amount is normally based on the number of feet along the front of your lot, hence the term front foot fee. On a typical house, this fee can vary. The bill is paid annually, and when the home is sold, the expense transfers to the new homeowner.

Question: Emblements are:
Feedback / Explanation: Emblements are annual crops, produced by cultivation, which legally belong to a tenant with an implied right of harvest. They are treated as the tenant's personal property.

Question: What type of title insurance policy covers everything?
D: No title insurance policy covers everything
Feedback / Explanation: Be careful of absolute words such as words like ALL, EVERYTHING, ALWAYS or NEVER- these words rarely apply to real estate concepts.

Question: A broker shows a prospective buyer a home. The buyer makes an offer and the seller accepts. The broker, buyer, and seller then go to the Reliable Escrow Company where the buyer makes out a personal check for a $1,000 deposit payable to Reliable Escrow Company and hands it to the escrow officer. Which of the following is correct?
A: The broker has to keep a record of all checks deposited into his personal bank account
B: No record is required to be kept since the check was made out directly the escrow company and was not delivered by the broker
C: The broker has to keep a record of the check in his record of all trust funds received and not placed in his trust account
D: The broker can keep a digital record of the check on his phone only, as long as the governing real estate body knows where his phone is kept
Feedback / Explanation: Brokers are generally required to keep records of all financial dealings related to real estate transactions they are brokering.

Question: Roberto owns an apartment building and wants to exchange his property in a 1031 tax deferred exchange in order to defer paying taxes in the year of the exchange. He could exchange his apartment building for:
A: a personal residence with an existing

Question: Which of the following is required for a valid deed?
C: Eviction on any day of the week except on Sundays
D: Use of the phrase to have and to hold
Feedback / Explanation: Property deeds are used to convey real property from a grantor (seller) to a grantee (buyer). For a deed to be legally operative, several essentials must exist including the identification of the grantor and grantee, and an adequate legal description of the property.

Question: The unadjusted basis of a taxpayer's residence would be its:
Feedback / Explanation: For federal income tax purposes in determining gain or loss on real property, the term "unadjusted basis" most nearly means "original cost". NOTE - This question asks about "unadjusted basis", not "adjusted basis". Sometimes you may also see unadjusted basis simply referred to as "basis"

Question: Which of the following is treated as a credit on the seller's closing statement?
A: none of the other options are correct
Feedback / Explanation: If a seller has already paid for a period of property tax that must be reimbursed by the buyer, it is a credit on the seller's closing statement. Prepaid premiums, such as insurance premiums paid in advance by the seller, are not typically treated as a credit on the seller's closing statement because these payments are specific to the seller’s policy and coverage period. When a property is sold, the buyer will usually obtain their own insurance policy effective as of the closing date. Therefore, any prepaid insurance premiums would not be credited to the seller in the transaction. Prepaid rents from tenants are actually credited to the buyer, not the seller, on a closing statement. If tenants have paid rent in advance for a period that extends past the closing date, the buyer is entitled to this rent, as they will be the property owner during that time.

Question: An installment sale represents a tax advantage because:
B: it defers payment of capital gains.
Feedback / Explanation: An installment sale allows the taxpayer to postpone the receipt and reporting of income tax to future years when his/her other income may be lower. Thus, the seller of a parcel of land in a land installment contract can avoid paying the entire tax on the gain in the year of sale.

Question: Boot refers to:
Feedback / Explanation: The term "boot" is not used in the Internal Revenue Code or the Regulations, but is commonly used in discussing the tax consequences of a Section 1031 tax-deferred exchange. For example, if a property worth $150,000 is exchanged for a property worth $145,000 plus $5000 cash, the cash is considered the boot. Receipt of boot doesn't disqualify an exchange, but it may result in a taxable gain.

Question: Personal income taxes are structured using rates that are:
Feedback / Explanation: Progressive means the more you make, the more you pay. It might also be termed "graduated."

Question: The selling price on a buyer's closing statement is treated as:
C: None of the other options are correct
Feedback / Explanation: A closing statement is a detailed cash accounting of a real estate transaction prepared by a broker, escrow officer, attorney or other persons designated to process the mechanics of the sales, showing all cash received, all charges and credits made and all cash paid out in the transaction. Selling price on the buyer's closing statement is treated as a debit to the buyer.

Question: To determine property taxes on a residence, an assessor will:
A: total all land and improvements and assess them together, then multiply each by the different tax rates for land and improvements.
B: assess land and improvements separately, then multiply the total by one tax rate.
C: total all land and improvements and assess them together, then multiply by one tax rate.
D: assess land and improvements separately, then multiply each by the different tax rates for land and improvements.
Feedback / Explanation: Land and improvements are assessed separately, then added together and multiplied by one tax rate.

Question: Under the federal Lead-Based Paint Hazard Reduction Act, which statement is TRUE?
A: All residential housing built prior to 1978 must be tested for the presence of lead-based paint before being listed for sale or rent.
B: A lead hazard pamphlet must be distributed to all prospective buyers but not to tenants.
C: Purchasers of housing built before 1978 must be given five days to test the property for the presence of lead-based paint.
D: A disclosure statement must be included with all sales contracts and leases involving residential properties built prior to 1978.
Feedback / Explanation: Although no remediation of the property is required, a lead-based paint warning disclosure statement must be completed and signed by the parties and a copy included with any sales contract or lease for a residential property built before 1978. In the disclosure statement, buyers and renters acknowledge receiving a HUD booklet about protecting their families from lead-based paint hazards. The pamphlet "Protect Your Family From Lead in the Home" must be provided to lessees as well as to buyers. The length of time given the buyer to inspect the property is listed in the lead-based paint disclosure provided by the seller.

Question: All of the following are examples of private controls of real property EXCEPT:
B: a requirement to make monthly payments to maintain common elements within the complex.
C: a condition stating the property may not be used to sell alcohol.
D: criteria that establish the color of house paint that can be used.
Feedback / Explanation: Certificates of occupancy are public control created by zoning laws. Deed conditions, Owner association or cooperative fees and covenants on paint color are all private controls.

Question: An example of involuntary alienation of property is
Feedback / Explanation: Condemnation is the operation of a government to take private property for public use and is an involuntary transfer of the property. Property inherited or given as a gift transfers voluntarily. A quitclaim deed voluntarily transfers the interest a grantor may have in a property to a grantee with no covenants or warranties.

Question: General real estate property taxes levied for the operation of the government are called:
Feedback / Explanation: The general real estate property taxes, the "ad valorem" tax, creates a specific lien on a particular property. The word "general" in this question denotes that this tax is different from a special assessment tax (which is levied on real estate to fund a public improvement that also benefits the property being taxed). Ad Valorem means "according to value", and an ad valorem tax is a tax based on the assessed value of an item. The most common ad valorem taxes are property taxes levied on real estate.

Question: The construction of a family room, additional bedroom, and extra bath has been completed on the owner's home. Before the addition can be used, which of the following is TRUE?
A: The bath must be inspected by the plumbing inspector.
B: A certificate of occupancy must be issued.
C: The municipality must issue a building permit.
D: The municipality must issue a conditional-use permit.
Feedback / Explanation: A certificate of occupancy grants permission to occupy a completed building structure after it has been inspected. A building inspector may inspect the plumbing during the actual building process before completion. A building permit is issued before construction begins. A conditional use permit does not relate to actual building construction but permits use of a property for a use related to, but not permitted by, zoning ordinances.

Question: An annual crop that is considered personal property is called a(n)
Feedback / Explanation: Annually cultivated crops such as fruit, vegetables, and grains are known as emblements and are considered personal property. A fixture is an item that was once personal property, but that has been attached or affixed to real estate and has become real property. A fixture is tangible and transfers with the property unless detached from the property prior to its sale. An appurtenance is anything that attaches to and runs with the land such as improvements, rights and fixtures.

Question: A "chain of title" is:
A: the list of all parties who have ever owned real estate in a county.
B: an involuntary conveyance of title by statutory rules of descent.
C: a chronology of successive owners of record of a parcel of real estate.
D: the bundle of rights linked to the recorded title to a parcel.
Feedback / Explanation: A "chain of title" refers to the series of owners of record relating to a specific property, dating back to the original grant of title from the state to a private party. It is a chronological list (as shown in title records).

Question: A competent and disinterested person who is authorized by another person to act in his or her place and sign a contract of sale is called:
Feedback / Explanation: An attorney-in-fact is the person named in a power of attorney document. An attorney-in-fact is a fiduciary of the person they are representing, so they must remain "disinterested" - acting in an objective manner without any hidden motivation or prospect of gain. For example, a person who made a secret deal to sign a contract contrary to his client's best interests in exchange for an under-the-table payment would not be "disinterested."

Question: When a prospective buyer makes a written purchase offer that the seller accepts, then the:
A: Buyer receives legal title to the property.
B: Seller grants the buyer ownership rights.
C: Buyer receives equitable title to the property.
D: Buyer may take possession of the real estate.
Feedback / Explanation: "Equitable title" means that the prospective buyer has obtained the right to acquire ownership of a property currently owned and occupied by another.

Question: A person agrees to sell a property for $500,000. The buyer gives the seller $150 as valuable consideration for a six-month option. Which of the following statements is true?
A: The buyer must have at least 5% down as valuable consideration
B: The seller cannot accept money for the option
C: The $150 is valuable consideration if the seller accepts it
D: The buyer must have at least 20% down
Feedback / Explanation: "Valuable consideration" is a necessary component of all contracts. It is the benefit one party receives in exchange for granting benefit to the other. Generally it is money in any amount both parties agree to, though it can take other forms such as personal property, work or refraining from an act. If the seller of a home accepts $150 to keep an option to buy open on the property, it will be considered the "consideration" necessary to form a valid contract.

Question: What is required if personal property is used as consideration in the purchase of real property?
B: A current appraisal of the personal property
Feedback / Explanation: A bill of sale is a document that transfers ownership of goods (personal property) from one person to another.

Question: The primary purpose of a deed is to:
Feedback / Explanation: A deed is the instrument by which ownership of a property is transferred from one person to another, while a title is evidence of that ownership. A title is evidence of ownership, a deed is evidence of the transfer.

Question: A borrower defaulted on his home mortgage loan payments, and the lender obtained a court order to foreclose on the property. At the foreclosure sale, the property sold for $164,000; the unpaid balance on the loan at the time of foreclosure was $178,000. What must the lender do to recover the $14,000 that the borrower still owes?
Feedback / Explanation: A deficiency judgment is an unsecured money judgment against a borrower whose mortgage foreclosure sale did not produce sufficient funds to pay the underlying promissory note, or loan, in full.

Question: Which of the following may appear on a closing statement as a credit to the buyer?
A: Property taxes after the closing date of March 31st until June 30th
B: Property taxes after November 15th to the date of closing
D: The balance of an assumed loan
Feedback / Explanation: A loan assumption is when a buyer takes over the seller’s loan. Since the buyer does not need to bring that loan amount to closing, it appears as a credit to the buyer.

Question: Which of the following would be used to clear a defect from the title records?
Feedback / Explanation: A suit to quiet title is used to correct mistakes or disputes in the property title, thereby “quieting” other claims.

Question: On a settlement statement, the cost of the required lender’s title insurance policy is usually shown as a:
Feedback / Explanation: The buyer must purchase lender’s title insurance, so it appears as a debit (money owed) on the buyer’s side of the settlement statement.

Question: A deed of conveyance contained only the following guarantee: “This property was not encumbered during the time the grantor owned it except as noted in this deed.” What type of deed did the grantor give to the grantee?
Feedback / Explanation: A special warranty deed guarantees against title defects only during the grantor’s ownership, not before.

Question: The best way to discover a flaw in the recorded title to a piece of real property is by:
Feedback / Explanation: A title search ensures the seller has the legal right to sell and checks for liens or encumbrances.

Question: If a developer wants to build a commercial building closer to the street than is permitted by the local zoning ordinance because the shape of the lot makes a standard setback impossible, the developer should seek a
Feedback / Explanation: A variance allows deviation from zoning rules when strict compliance is impractical.

Question: Joe conveys property to Vivian by delivering a deed. The deed contains six covenants. This is MOST likely a:
Feedback / Explanation: A warranty deed includes six covenants that guarantee clear title and the right to transfer.

Question: Which of the following statements concerning the addition or subtraction of land is correct?
A: Accretion is the gradual deposit of soil through natural causes
B: Accretion is the gradual loss of soil through natural causes
C: Erosion is the natural wear and tear of property improvements
D: Erosion is the gradual deposit of soil due to ocean tides
Feedback / Explanation: Accretion refers to gradual deposits of soil by natural forces, increasing land area.

Question: The right of alienation is defined as:
B: the right to make a will
C: the right to transfer an interest in real property
D: the right to execute and deliver a deed
Feedback / Explanation: Alienation is the owner’s right to transfer property, whether by sale, gift, or will.

Question: Three years after signing a five-year lease, business A outgrew its rental space. Business A would like business B to take on the responsibility of the lease for the remaining two years. Business B can do this through:
Feedback / Explanation: An assignment transfers the rights and obligations of a lease to another party, with landlord consent.

Question: Which of the following best describes earnest money?
A: The consideration for the sale of the property
C: The money provided by the purchaser at the time of entering into a contract
D: The money deposited by the purchaser with the broker or seller to pay for the expenses of examining the title
Feedback / Explanation: Earnest money is a good faith deposit showing the buyer’s intent to complete the purchase.

Question: Which of the following could not be real property?
Feedback / Explanation: A mortgage is a personal obligation, not real property. Mineral rights, easements, and fixtures are real property.

Question: The covenant in a deed that states that the grantor is the owner and has the right to convey the title is called the:
Feedback / Explanation: The covenant of seisin guarantees the grantor holds title and has the right to transfer it.

Question: Unless there is a deed restriction that states otherwise, the holder of a life estate may do all of the following to their estate except:
Feedback / Explanation: A life estate ends at death and cannot be willed to heirs, though it can be rented, sold, or mortgaged.

Question: Until a land contract is paid in full, what is the status of the purchaser’s interest in the property?
A: Purchaser holds legal title to the premises
B: Purchaser possesses a legal life estate in the premises
C: Purchaser has equitable title in the property
D: Purchaser has no legal interest in the property
Feedback / Explanation: Under a land contract, the buyer has equitable title (use and possession), while the seller retains legal title until full payment.

Question: When a claim is settled by a title insurance company, the company acquires all rights and claims of the insured against any other person who is responsible for the loss. This is known as what?
Feedback / Explanation: Subrogation means the title insurer takes over the insured’s legal rights to pursue recovery from a responsible third party.

Question: On a closing statement in a typical real estate transaction, the buyer’s earnest money deposit is reflected as a:
B: credit to the seller and a debit to the buyer
Feedback / Explanation: Earnest money is applied toward the purchase price, so it appears as a credit to the buyer.

Question: When a buyer assumes a mortgage, prepaid insurance and tax reserves will appear on a typical closing statement as a:
A: credit to the buyer and a debit to the seller
D: debit to the buyer and a credit to the seller
Feedback / Explanation: The seller has already paid these, so the buyer must reimburse the seller (debit to buyer, credit to seller).

Question: On a settlement statement, prorations for real estate taxes paid in arrears are shown as a:
A: debit to the seller and a credit to the buyer
B: credit to both the seller and the buyer
C: debit to both the seller and the buyer
D: credit to the seller and a debit to the buyer
Feedback / Explanation: Taxes paid in arrears are owed by the seller up to the closing date, so they appear as a debit to the seller and credit to the buyer.

Question: Transferring a property's title over to another person can also be called:
Feedback / Explanation: In property law, alienation is the voluntary act of an owner of some property transferring, or otherwise disposing, of the property.

Question: The sudden tearing away or removal of land by action of water is:
Feedback / Explanation: In real property law, avulsion refers to a sudden loss or addition to land, which results from the action of water. It differs from erosion, which describes a gradual loss or addition to land resulting from the action of water. NOTE - Avulsion is SUDDEN, while erosion is GRADUAL. Accretion the process of growth or increase.

Question: A defect or a cloud on title to property may be cured by:
A: bringing an action to register the title.
B: obtaining proof of satisfaction from all appropriate parties.
C: paying cash for the property at the settlement.
D: bringing an action to repudiate the title.
Feedback / Explanation: In United States property law, a cloud on title or title defect refers to any irregularity in the chain of title for a property that would give a reasonable person pause before accepting a conveyance of title. It can be cured by obtaining proof of satisfaction between the affected parties, or by a "quiet title" order.

Question: If Joe dies without having made a will, it is said to be:
Feedback / Explanation: Intestacy is the condition of the estate of a person who dies without having made a valid will or other binding declaration. Alternatively this may also apply where a will or declaration has been made, but only applies to part of the estate; the remaining estate forms the "intestate estate".

Question: Carol enters into a sale-leaseback agreement with Joe, under which Joe will become the owner of Carol’s ranch. Which of these statements is TRUE of this arrangement?
A: Joe receives possession of the property
D: Carol retains title to the ranch
Feedback / Explanation: A sale-leaseback (short for "sale-and-lease-back") is a financial transaction in which one sells an asset and leases it back for the long term. This allows one to be able to use the asset, but no longer own it and be responsible for the costs associated with owning it.

Question: What do liens and easements have in common?
B: Both must be on public record to be valid
C: Neither can be done without the consent of the owner
D: Both are money claims against the property
Feedback / Explanation: Liens are, of course, serious in that they indicate the owner has failed to pay a debt secured directly or indirectly by the property. Easements, on the other hand, are a practical necessity for many residential properties. Both, however, result in an encumbrance on the affected property.

Question: When the grantor does not wish to convey certain property rights, he or she:
A: may note the exceptions in the deed of conveyance.
B: must note the exceptions in a separate document.
C: may not do so, since the deed conveys the entire premises.
D: must convey the entire premises and have the grantee reconvey the rights to be retained by the grantor.
Feedback / Explanation: An Exception in Deed is a statement in a deed that reserves certain rights for the grantor- for example, if the grantor wants to sell a property but keep the mineral rights in the land.

Question: On a settlement statement, the selling price is ALWAYS:
Feedback / Explanation: On a closing statement for a real estate sale, a debit is an item that is charged to a party. The selling price is the point of reference for all debits and credits on a closing statement, and it is always a debit (debt to be paid) to the buyer.

Question: A bill of sale is used to transfer the ownership of what?
Feedback / Explanation: Personal property differs from "real property" in a number of respects, most importantly its portability. Cars, furniture, clothing, paintings, jewelry, appliances and just about any other non-food item one buys are examples of personal property. A bill-of-sale is proof of the transfer of ownership of personal property.

Question: The recording of a deed:
A: insures the interest in a parcel of real estate.
B: is all that is required to transfer the title to real estate.
C: gives constructive notice of the ownership of real property.
D: warrants the title to real property.
Feedback / Explanation: Recording a deed does not convey, insure or warrant ownership. However, it does protect the owner's interest in a property by serving notice that the recorded owner is the only recognized holder of title. This places a larger burden of proof and process on someone trying to assert a prior ownership interest and/or claiming a deedholder's title is clouded.

Question: To rehabilitate real property usually means:
A: the property will be restored to a good condition.
B: the property must be replaced to obtain its highest and best use potential.
C: design changes must be made to the property.
D: the use of the property will be changed.
Feedback / Explanation: Rehabilitation of property means to restore real property to an improved state. The restoration is usually needed because the property's condition has worsened.

Question: What kind of lien is created as a result of a judgment, estate or inheritance taxes, the decedent’s debts, or unpaid federal income taxes?
Feedback / Explanation: A tax lien or judgment lien attaches to all personal and real property of a person or firm (the "lienee"), which makes it a general lien. The attachment of judgement liens to personal property can vary state-by-state but is recognized at the national level.

Question: Which lien has priority in a foreclosure sale?
D: Mechanics or materialmen who file a timely notice after commencing work on the property
Feedback / Explanation: Tax liens take priority over all other liens, including liens recorded prior to a tax lien.

Question: When real estate under an estate for years agreement is sold, what happens to the lease?
D: It is subject to termination with proper notice
Feedback / Explanation: Tenancy for years is the common form of rental agreements, and it generally binds all future owners of the property for the term of the lease.

Question: Stephen, his nephew, and his niece are joint tenants. Stephen sells his interest to his sister, and then his nephew dies. As a result, which of these statements is TRUE?
A: The nephew’s heirs are joint tenants with Stephen and his sister
B: Stephen’s niece and sister are tenants in common
C: Stephen's niece and sister are joint tenants
D: The nephew’s heirs and Stephen are joint tenants, but the sister is a tenant in common
Feedback / Explanation: Tenancy in Common is ownership of real property by two or more parties that hold an interest in the property- they share only possession, not necessarily time, title, or interest equality. Joint Tenancy is an interest in property taken by two or more joint tenants with equal interest, sharing the same title and possession, and beginning at the same time. Upon the death of a joint tenant, the interest passes to the surviving joint tenants rather than to the heirs of the deceased. NOTE - When Stephen's nephew dies, his share passes to the niece. But because the sister's interest didn't begin at the same time as the niece's, they can't be Joint Tenants- so their common ownership reverts to Tenancy in Common.

Question: Joe and Susan own property as joint tenants. Susan then sells her interest to Carol. What is the relationship between Joe and Carol regarding the property?
B: No relationship exists because Susan cannot sell her joint tenancy interest
Feedback / Explanation: If one joint tenant sells or transfers their interest to a third party, the joint tenancy is typically converted into a tenancy in common, as the right of survivorship only applies to the original joint tenants.

Question: A purchaser obtains financing from a local credit union to purchase a condominium unit. In this situation, which of these BEST describes the purchaser?
Feedback / Explanation: The mortgagor is the borrower in a mortgage, typically a homeowner. The borrower gives the lender a mortgage, which can be considered a "pledge" to pay the borrowed money back, so the lender is the "mortgagee."

Question: If someone has a license to cross private property, which of the following would cause the license to terminate?
A: The person with the license had not used it for six months
C: The property had a tax lien filed against it
D: The person with the license had not used it for one year
Feedback / Explanation: If a property was sold, it would terminate a license for crossing a private property because the issuer of the license (the previous property owner) would no longer be involved. The new owner would need to grant a new license to the person who wants to cross the private property.

Question: What is the difference between a general lien and a specific lien?
A: A specific lien is a lien against a certain parcel of real estate, while a general lien covers all of a debtor’s property.
B: A general lien cannot be enforced in court, while a specific lien can.
C: A specific lien is held by only one person, while a general lien must be held by two or more people.
D: A general lien is a lien against personal property, while a specific lien is a lien against real estate.
Feedback / Explanation: A general lien is against all the property owned by a debtor. This is different from a specific lien, which is against a specific piece of property. With a specific lien, the specific piece of property alone satisfies the debt- the lien does not attach to other property owned by the debtor, as it does with a general lien.

Question: "Boot” is a term used in a 1031 Exchange when which of the following happens?
A: When there is a difference between the equity of properties being exchanged
B: When depreciating an income property for tax purposes
C: When taxable income is more than the true value of the property
D: When straight line depreciation is used for an income property
Feedback / Explanation: The term "boot" is not used in the Internal Revenue Code or the Regulations, but is commonly used in discussing the tax consequences of a Section 1031 tax-deferred exchange. For example, if a property worth $150,000 is exchanged for a property worth $145,000 plus $5000 cash, the cash is considered the boot. Receipt of boot doesn't disqualify an exchange, but it may result in a taxable gain.

Question: The term "boot" would probably be considered in connection with a(n):
Feedback / Explanation: The term "boot" is not used in the Internal Revenue Code or the Regulations, but is commonly used in discussing the tax consequences of a Section 1031 tax-deferred exchange. For example, if a property worth $150,000 is exchanged for a property worth $145,000 plus $5000 cash, the cash is considered the boot. Receipt of boot doesn't disqualify an exchange, but it may result in a taxable gain.

Question: In most states, by paying the debt after a foreclosure sale, the mortgagor has the right to regain the property. What is this right called?
Feedback / Explanation: In most states, after a foreclosure sale, the mortgagor (the person who took out the mortgage) may have the right to regain the property by paying off the debt, including any additional costs and interest. This right is known as the "statutory right of redemption." It is defined by state law rather than arising out of the mortgage contract itself (which makes it "statutory"). The statutory right of redemption allows the original property owner a specified amount of time after the foreclosure sale to redeem the property by paying the sale price and any other charges. The duration of the redemption period varies by state, and not all states offer this right. This right exists to protect homeowners from losing their property for less than its fair market value and to provide an additional opportunity to retain their property even after foreclosure. Equitable Right of Redemption allows the mortgagor (the borrower) to redeem the property before the foreclosure sale takes place. It is based on equitable principles, meaning it's recognized as a fair opportunity for the borrower to keep their property by paying off the full amount owed, including any interest and fees, up until the time of the foreclosure auction or sale. The equitable right of redemption exists in virtually all states and is available up until the foreclosure sale. The purpose is to give the borrower a last chance to save their property by settling their debts.

Question: The cost of title insurance is based primarily on:
B: any number of covenants in the warranty deed.
Feedback / Explanation: Title insurance protects a lender or borrower from financial loss due to defects in a property's title, for example if a deed or deed of trust is fraudulent, lost, or incorrectly filed. The cost of title insurance varies mostly based on a property's value.

Question: Which of the following provides a buyer with the best assurance of clear, marketable title?
Feedback / Explanation: Of the answers provided, title insurance provides the best assurance of marketable title.

Question: Voluntary alienation refers to:
Feedback / Explanation: Voluntary alienation is the transfer of real property (or some other asset) through a person's intentional actions and of their own free will.

Question: If a buyer agrees to purchase a home "as is":
A: None of the other options are correct.
B: neither the seller nor the agent is required to reveal known defects.
C: the agent must reveal known defects to the buyer.
D: known defects are irrelevant to an "as is" purchase.
Feedback / Explanation: "As is" means the home is being sold in its present condition, but it does not mean that the buyer must agree to buy the home without investigating any potential problems. Generally, states require material fact property disclosures be provided to buyers, regardless of the type of sale agreement.

Question: In a settlement statement, how will a proration of prepaid water, gas, and electric charges be reflected?
A: As a debit to the buyer only
B: As a debit to the buyer and a credit to the seller
C: As a credit to the seller only
D: As a debit to the seller and a credit to the buyer
Feedback / Explanation: In this situation, prepaid utility charges would show as a credit to the seller (because the seller has already paid for them) and a debit to the buyer (because the buyer needs to pay the seller back for them).

Question: An interest in real estate is best defined as ownership of
A: the full bundle of rights to real property
C: one or more of the bundle of rights to real property
D: the right to possession and use of real property
Feedback / Explanation: An interest in real estate is ownership of any combination of the bundle of sticks, including the rights to possess, use, transfer, encumber and exclude

Question: Encumbrances and police powers are
A: interests that do not include possession
Feedback / Explanation: An encumbrance enables a non-owning party to restrict the owner's bundle of rights. Government entities also have nonpossessory interests in real estate which act to control land use for the public good within the entity's jurisdiction. The prime example of public interest is police power

Question: What distinguishes a freehold estate from a leasehold estate?
A: A freehold includes the right to dispose or use
B: A leasehold endures only for a specific period of time
D: A leasehold is subject to government restrictions
Feedback / Explanation: Freehold estates are ownership estates, and their duration cannot be determined. A leasehold endures only for a specific period of time

Question: The highest form of ownership interest one can acquire in real estate is the
Feedback / Explanation: The fee simple freehold estate is the highest form of ownership interest one can acquire in real estate. It includes the complete bundle of rights, and the tenancy is unlimited, with certain exceptions. The fee simple absolute estate is a perpetual estate that is not conditioned by stipulated or restricted uses

Question: The distinguishing feature of a defeasible fee simple estate is that
A: it can be passed on to heirs
B: it has no restrictions on use
C: the estate may revert to a grantor or heirs if the prescribed use changes
Feedback / Explanation: The defeasible fee estate is perpetual, provided the usage conforms to stated conditions. Essential characteristics are: the property must be used for a certain purpose or under certain conditions; and, if the use changes or if prohibited conditions are present, the estate reverts to the previous grantor of the estate

Question: Upon the death of the owner, a life estate passes to
A: the original owner or other named person
Feedback / Explanation: Upon the death of the owner or other named individual, the estate passes to the original owner or another named party

Question: How is a conventional life estate created?
A: It happens automatically when title transfers unless a fee simple is specifically claimed
B: A fee simple owner grants the life estate to a life tenant
C: It is created by judicial action
D: It is created by a statutory period of adverse possession
Feedback / Explanation: A conventional life estate is created by grant from a fee simple property owner to the grantee, the life tenant

Question: What distinguishes a pur autre vie life estate from an ordinary life estate?
A: The pur autre vie estate endures only for the lifetime of the grantor
B: The pur autre vie estate endures only for the lifetime of the grantee
C: The pur autre vie estate endures only for the lifetime of a person other than the grantee
D: The pur autre vie estate cannot revert to the grantor
Feedback / Explanation: A pur autre vie life estate endures over the lifetime of a third person, after which the property passes from the tenant holder to the original grantor (reversion) or a third party (remainderman)

Question: Which of the following life estates is created by someone other than the owner?
Feedback / Explanation: A legal life estate is created by state law as opposed to being created by a property owner's agreement

Question: Which of the following is true of a homestead?
A: A homestead interest cannot be conveyed by one spouse
B: A homestead interest cannot be passed to the children of the head of household
C: A homestead interest is a form of conventional life estate
D: A homestead is a primary or secondary residence occupied by a family
Feedback / Explanation: A homestead is one's principal residence. Homestead laws protect family members against losing their homes to general creditors attempting to collect on debts. Homestead laws generally provide that: the homestead interest cannot be conveyed by one spouse; both spouses must sign the deed conveying homestead property

Question: Dower refers to
A: joint tenancy of husband and wife
B: a wife's life estate interest in her husband's property
D: a child's life estate interest in his or her parents' homestead
Feedback / Explanation: Dower refers to a wife's life estate interest in the husband's property. When the husband dies, the wife can make a claim to portions of the decedent's property. Curtesy is the identical right enjoyed by the husband in a deceased wife's property. Property acquired under dower laws is owned by the surviving spouse for the duration of his or her lifetime

Question: Which of the following is an illustration of the legal concept of elective share?
A: A surviving spouse places a lien on a debtor's property
B: A widow who was excluded from a will makes a claim to a portion of the couple's principal residence
C: A spouse who loses her home because of her husband's gambling debt sues in court for exemption from the debt
D: A widower whose spouse died without a will sues to change the provisions of the will
Feedback / Explanation: Elective share is a state-level statute enabling a surviving spouse to make a minimum claim to the deceased spouse's real and personal property in place of the provisions for such property in the decedent's will

Question: A one-year lease on a house has expired, but the tenant continues sending monthly rent checks to the owner, and the owner accepts them. What kind of leasehold estate exists?
Feedback / Explanation: In an estate from period-to-period, also called a periodic tenancy, the tenancy period automatically renews for an indefinite period of time, subject to timely payment of rent. At the end of a tenancy period, if the landlord accepts another regular payment of rent, the leasehold is considered to be renewed for another period

Question: Which of the following scenarios represents an owner’s undivided fractional interest in a property?
A: A local municipality is condemning an 8-story building
B: Condominium owners allocate three floors as a designated parking space
C: A general contractor encumbered the property when the condominium owner failed to pay
D: A mortgagor conveys legal title to the mortgagee
Feedback / Explanation: The undivided interest represents an owner’s interest when there are two or more parties in a shared estate. It does not reflect the physical division of the property

Question: Which of the following represents the highest form of estate ownership?
Feedback / Explanation: Properties conveyed as Fee Simple Absolute estates are considered the highest form of ownership because the heirs can dispose of, enjoy, exclude, control, and possess the property generally without restriction

Question: Which of the following distinguishes a determinable estate from a condition subsequent estate?
A: A determinable estate focuses on the use of the estate
B: The dispute of a determinable estate is settled in court
C: The recipient of a determinable estate determines the use of the estate
D: The remainderman of the determinable estate inherits the property
Feedback / Explanation: In a fee simple defeasible estate, the use of the property is established by the owner through a determinable estate. Should the property not be used according to the owner’s instructions, the property will automatically revert back to the grantor

Question: Which of the following reflects an estate restricted by specific dates or duration?
Feedback / Explanation: A leasehold estate refers to rental properties, whereby a lessor transfers possession of a premises to a lessee for a definitive period of time

Question: Which of the following is a right provided to a life tenant?
Feedback / Explanation: The life tenant holds the rights of possession during a life estate and reverts to the original owner or to another designated individual upon death

Question: What type of leasehold estate does not have a definitive end date?
Feedback / Explanation: An estate at will continues indefinitely and terminates when either party gives sufficient notice or upon death

Question: Which of the following statements describes a circumstance that involves an owner’s use of homestead rights?
A: An owner sells the property to a neighbor
B: An owner is forced to sell his or her principal residence to satisfy unsecured creditors
C: An owner is selling the property by voluntary alienation
D: The grantor is creating a life estate for an elderly parent
Feedback / Explanation: The homestead right is a form of protection against unsatisfied creditors attempting to collect a debt

Question: How do fee simple estates differ from life estates?
B: Life estates transfer assets only between family members
C: All fee simple estates are conditional
D: Fee simple estates have fewer rights than life estates
Feedback / Explanation: Ownership rights in fee simple estates include the right to dispose of the property

Question: What type of leasehold allows a tenant to renew the terms automatically?
Feedback / Explanation: The lease automatically renews upon acceptance of rent from the tenant to the landlord

Question: Mr. Brady left farmland for his nephew, allowing him to grow any vegetation. The only rule imposed by Mr. Brady is prohibiting the use of any pesticides or chemicals added to the soil.  Unfortunately, his nephew used chemicals and other products to accelerate growth. Because there was a violation of the conditions of the fee simple defeasible estate, Mr. Brady wishes to repossess the farmland.  Which type of fee simple defeasible estate will allow Mr. Brady to repossess the property due to a violation of conditions?
Feedback / Explanation: Mr. Brady placed a condition to not use pesticides or chemicals.  Because this condition was violated, Mr. Brady may repossess the estate.  In a fee simple defeasible estate, condition subsequent, if any condition is violated, the previous owner may repossess the property. However, reversion of the estate is not automatic: the grantor must re-take physical possession within a certain time frame

Question: Charles created a life estate for his mother. Upon his mother’s death, Charles will have his daughter Mary move into the condominium where his mother lived. Mary, who will have a future interest, is known as the
Feedback / Explanation: If a life estate names a third party to receive title to the property upon termination of the life estate, the party who has a future interest is called a remainderman

Question: Lisa grants a life estate for Caregiver Joanne, over the lifetime of Lisa’s mom. Upon the death of Lisa’s mom, Lisa grants her niece the right to receive the property. What type of conventional life estate did Lisa create?
Feedback / Explanation: The pur autre vie life estate extends over the lifetime of a third person, after which the property passes from the tenant holder to the original grantor (reversion) or a third party (remainderman)

Question: A 3-story residential property is being considered for eminent domain. The landlord has allowed the current tenants to reside until the property has been condemned. What type of a leasehold estate did both parties agree to?
Feedback / Explanation: Also known as tenancy at will, an estate at will has no definite expiration date or “renewal” cycle, so long as the rent is paid timely and other lease conditions are met

Question: Karen signed an estate for years with Draper Apartments. As the lessee, what type of rights will she have under this leasehold estate?
Feedback / Explanation: Leasehold tenants are entitled to possess and use the leased premises during the lease term in the manner prescribed in the lease

Question: The lessor and lessee signed a 1-year lease agreement.  Upon the expiration of the lease, the lessee failed to leave the apartment and continued to occupy the property without paying rent or obtaining the landlord’s consent. This rental situation is an example of which of the following?
Feedback / Explanation: An estate at sufferance occurs when the lessor and lessee have an agreement initially, but  the lessee continues to occupy the property after lease expiration without the consent of the landlord

Question: Municipalities have the right to enact ordinances and zoning laws for the public good within their jurisdiction.  This governmental power is known as
Feedback / Explanation: Police power, or the right of the local or county government to zone, is an enabling act whereby government powers are transferred to the local municipality to control land use for public good within the entity’s jurisdiction

Question: A freehold estate of potentially unlimited duration is a
Feedback / Explanation: A freehold estate of potentially unlimited duration is a fee simple estate: an estate limited to the life of the owner is a life estate

Question: A legal life estate is created by
Feedback / Explanation: A legal life estate is created by state law as opposed to being created by a property owner's agreement. Provisions vary from state to state. The focus of a legal life estate is defining and protecting the property rights of surviving family members upon the death of the husband or wife

Question: The principal interests accompanying the legal life estate are the
A: remainderman, reversion and pur autre vie
D: homestead, dower and curtesy, and elective share
Feedback / Explanation: The principal interests conveyable in a legal life estate are the homestead, dower and curtesy, and elective share

Question: Which of the following statements regarding the general provisions of the homestead laws is true?
A: The homestead exemption is also known as the homestead tax exemption, which exempts a portion of the property's value from taxation
B: The homestead interest can be conveyed by one spouse; both spouses do not need to sign the deed conveying the homestead property
C: The homestead laws can be applied to any investment property that that is owned by a married couple
D: The homestead laws protect family members against losing their homes to general creditors attempting to collect on debts
Feedback / Explanation: A homestead is one's principal residence. Homestead laws protect family members against losing their homes to general creditors attempting to collect on debts

Question: If a tenant with a leasehold estate for years remains in possession of the leased premises after lease expiration, a(n) ___________ is created.
Feedback / Explanation: If a tenant with a leasehold estate for years remains in possession of the leased premises after lease expiration, a holdover lease is created. In the absence of a new lease agreement stating otherwise, the holdover tenancy becomes a periodic tenancy.

Question: Two people own a house, each having an undivided equal interest.  Which of the following best describes what they own?
A: Each owns fifty percent of the physical house and the land it rests on
B: Each owns one hundred percent of the physical house and the land it rests on
C: Each owns fifty percent of the estate represented by the real property, but no physical portion of the real property
D: Each owns one hundred percent of the estate represented by the real property and fifty percent of the physical house and the land it rests on
Feedback / Explanation: An undivided interest is an owner's interest in a property in which two or more parties share ownership. The terms "undivided" and "indivisible" signify that the owner's interest is in a fractional part of the entire estate, not in a physical portion of the real property itself. If two co-owners have an undivided equal interest, one owner may not lay claim to the northern half of the property for his or her exclusive use

Question: An interest in real estate that includes the right to possess is considered
Feedback / Explanation: Interests are principally distinguished by whether they include possession. If the interest-holder enjoys the right of possession, the party is considered to have an estate in land, or, familiarly an estate

Question: An interest in real estate that does not include the right to possess is considered
Feedback / Explanation: Interests are principally distinguished by whether they include possession. If a private interest-holder does not have the right to possess, the interest is an encumbrance

Question: The right to zone land and the right to acquire ownership of land by eminent domain are both examples of
Feedback / Explanation: Interests are principally distinguished by whether they include possession. If the interest-holder is not private, such as a government entity, and does not have the right to possess, the interest is some form of public interest

Question: If the duration of an owner's rights in an estate cannot be determined, the owner possesses
Feedback / Explanation: Freehold estates are ownership estates, and their duration cannot be determined; that is to say that the ownership rights may endure for a lifetime, for less than a lifetime, or for generations beyond the owner's lifetime

Question: The distinguishing feature of a leasehold estate is
A: ownership of an interest by a tenant
B: temporary ownership of the full bundle of rights in a property
C: unlimited ownership of one right in the bundle of rights in a property
D: duration limited by a lease term
Feedback / Explanation: A leasehold estate is distinguished by its specific duration, as represented by the lease term. Further, leasehold tenants only enjoy limited property rights: use; temporary possession, and limited exclusion

Question: Which of the following is a freehold tenant?
A: The owner of a freehold estate
B: The lessee in a leased property
C: The lessee of a freehold estate
D: Any owner of an estate in land
Feedback / Explanation: The owner of the freehold estate is the freehold tenant

Question: In what kind of estate does ownership revert to the grantor of the estate if usage fails to conform to a stated condition in the grant?
Feedback / Explanation: The defeasible fee estate is perpetual, provided the usage conforms to stated conditions

Question: Bob grants his sister Mary an estate for as long as she lives, but her heirs cannot inherit the estate.  What kind of estate is it?
Feedback / Explanation: An ordinary life estate ends with the death of the life estate owner and may pass back to the original owners or their heirs (reversion) or to a named third party (remainder)

Question: Homestead, dower and curtesy, and elective share are examples of
C: an estate created by an owner's agreement
Feedback / Explanation: The major forms of legal life estate are the homestead, dower and curtesy, and elective share

Question: To convey clear title in a dower and curtesy state,
A: both spouses must release their interest to the other spouse
B: both spouses must sign the conveyance
C: there must be two separate conveyances, each signed by one spouse
D: the head of household must sign the conveyance
Feedback / Explanation: To transfer property within dower and curtesy states, the husband (or wife) must obtain a release of the dower interest from the other spouse in order to convey clear title to another party. If both parties sign the conveyance, the dower right is automatically extinguished

Question: States that do not have dower and curtesy usually have either community property laws or
Feedback / Explanation: Dower and curtesy laws have been largely supplanted by community property laws and elective share laws

Question: Which of the following rights is conveyed with a leasehold estate?
A: The right to exclude everyone from the premises
B: The right to encumber the fee interest
C: The right to sell the premises
D: The right to possess and use the premises
Feedback / Explanation: Leasehold tenants are entitled to possess and use the leased premises during the lease term in the manner prescribed in the lease

Question: An estate from period-to-period will continue as long as
A: the tenant makes, and landlord accepts, regular rent payments
B: the term specified in the lease
C: the period is less than a year
D: the landlord has not given notice of termination
Feedback / Explanation: In an estate from period-to-period, also called a periodic tenancy, the tenancy period automatically renews as long as the tenant pays rent in a timely manner and the landlord accepts it. At the end of a tenancy period, if the landlord accepts another regular payment of rent, the leasehold is considered to be renewed for another period. A conveyance of leased property does not cancel a leasehold interest

Question: An estate at will
B: is terminated only if so stated in the lessee's last will and testament
C: terminates on the death of lessor or lessee
D: terminates on the date specified in the lease agreement
Feedback / Explanation: The estate at will, also called a tenancy at will, has no definite expiration date and hence no "renewal" cycle. The landlord and tenant agree that the tenancy will have no specified termination date, provided rent is paid on time and other lease conditions are met. For example, a son leases a house to his father and mother "forever," or until they want to move. The estate at will is terminated by proper notice, or by the death of either party

Question: If a tenant occupies a leased property without the consent of the landlord after a lease expires, the type of estate that results is called
Feedback / Explanation: In an estate at sufferance, a tenant occupies the premises without consent of the landlord or other legal agreement with the landlord. Usually such an estate involves a tenant who fails to vacate at the expiration of the lease, continuing occupancy without any right to do so

Question: When a single individual or entity owns a fee or life estate in a real property, the type of ownership is
Feedback / Explanation: If a single party owns the fee or life estate, the ownership is a tenancy in severalty

Question: Three people have identical rights but unequal shares in a property, share an indivisible interest, and may sell or transfer their interest without consent of the others. This type of ownership is
Feedback / Explanation: Tenancy in common, co-tenants enjoy an individually owned, undivided interest; any ownership share possible; no survivorship

Question: The "four unities" required to create a joint tenancy include which of the following conditions?
A: Parties must acquire respective interests at the same time
B: Parties must be legally married at the time of acquiring interest
D: Parties must have joint financial resources
Feedback / Explanation: All owners must acquire the property at the same time, use the same deed, acquire equal interests, and share in equal rights of possession

Question: Unlike tenants in common, joint tenants
A: own distinct portions of the physical property
B: cannot will their interest to a party outside the tenancy
C: may own unequal shares of the property
D: cannot sell their interest to outside parties
Feedback / Explanation: The survivorship feature of joint tenancy presents an advantage to tenancy in common, in that interests pass without probate proceedings. On the other hand, joint tenants relinquish any ability to will their interest to parties outside of the tenancy

Question: In a community property state, a basic distinction is made between
A: property acquired together and property acquired separately over the duration of the marriage
B: property owned privately versus property owned by the state
C: property acquired during a marriage and property already owned by each party at the time of marriage
D: property acquired during the marriage and property acquired after the marriage
