Feedback / Explanation: Community property law distinguishes real and personal property into categories of separate and community property. Separate property belongs to one spouse; community property belongs to both spouses equally

Question: Who are the essential parties involved in an estate in trust?
Feedback / Explanation: In an estate in trust, a fee owner-- the grantor or trustor-- transfers legal title to a fiduciary-- the trustee-- who holds and manages the estate for the benefit of another party, the beneficiary

Question: The distinguishing features of a condominium estate are
A: ownership of a share in an association that owns one's apartment
B: tenancy in common interest in airspace and common areas of the property
C: fee simple ownership of the airspace in a unit and an undivided share of the entire property's common areas
D: fee simple ownership of a pro rata share of the entire property
Feedback / Explanation: Multi-unit residential or commercial properties. It combines ownership of a fee simple interest in the airspace within a unit with ownership of an undivided share, as a tenant in common

Question: Who owns the property in a time-share estate?
A: Ownership is shared by the developer and the broker
B: The property is owned by tenants in common or by a freehold owner who leases on a time-share basis
C: A real estate investment trust holds a fee simple estate
D: A general partner holds a fee simple interest and interval estates are owned by limited partners
Feedback / Explanation: Timeshare ownership is a fee or leasehold interest in a property whose owners or tenants agree to use the property on a periodic, non-overlapping basis

Question: Which of the following is true of a cooperative?
A: A cooperative may hold an owner liable for the unpaid operating expenses of other owners
B: The owners have a fee simple interest in the airspace of their respective apartments
C: Owners may retain their apartments even if they sell their stock in the cooperative
D: The proprietary lease is guaranteed to have a fixed rate of rent over the life of the lease term
Feedback / Explanation: Since the corporation owns an undivided interest in the cooperative property, debts and financial obligations apply to the property as a whole, not to individual units as in a condominium

Question: One difference between a cooperative estate and a condominium estate is that
A: a default by a coop owner may cause a foreclosure on the entire property instead of just a single unit, as with a condominium
B: the condominium owner must pay expenses as well as rent
C: the coop owner owns stock and a freehold real estate interest whereas the condominium owner simply owns real estate
D: the condominium owner owns the common elements and the airspace whereas the coop owner only owns the apartment
Feedback / Explanation: Should the cooperative corporation fail to meet its obligations, creditors and mortgagees may foreclose on the entire property. A completed foreclosure would terminate the shareholders' proprietary lease, and bankrupt the owning corporation. Compare this situation with that of a condominium, in which an individual's failure to pay endangers only that individual's unit, not the entire property

Question: A unique feature of a land trust is that
A: the trustee controls both the trustor and the beneficiary
B: the trustee takes ownership of both land and improvements
C: the identity of the beneficiary may not be identified
D: the properties in the trust are probated in the states where the properties are located
Feedback / Explanation: Beneficiary identity not on record - public records do not identify the beneficiary; the beneficiary owns and enjoys the property in secrecy

Question: If a single party owns the fee or life estate, the ownership is a
Feedback / Explanation: If a single party owns the fee or life estate, the ownership is a tenancy in severalty. Synonyms are sole ownership, ownership in severalty, and estate in severalty

Question: Ownership in severalty is synonymous with which of the following terms?
Feedback / Explanation: If a single party owns the fee or life estate, the ownership is a tenancy in severalty. Synonyms are sole ownership, ownership in severalty, and estate in severalty

Question: The most common form of co-ownership when the owners are not married is the
Feedback / Explanation: The tenancy in common, also known as the estate in common, is the most common form of co-ownership when the owners are not married

Question: Which of the following is a defining characteristic of tenancy in common?
Feedback / Explanation: two or more owners, identical rights, interests individually owned, electable ownership shares, no survivorship, no unity of time.

Question: Joint tenants may only convey their interests to outside parties as
Feedback / Explanation: In a joint tenancy, two or more persons collectively own a property as if they were a single person. Rights and interests are indivisible and equal: each has a shared interest in the whole property which cannot be divided up. Joint tenants may only convey their interests to outside parties as tenant-in-common interests. One cannot convey a joint tenant interest

Question: To create a joint tenancy, all owners must
A: occupy the property at the same time
C: own equal shares in the property
D: hold separate titles to their individual interests
Feedback / Explanation: Joint tenants own equal shares in the property, without exception. If there are four co-tenants, each owns 25% of the property. If there are ten co-tenants, each owns 10%

Question: A ____________ is a legal avenue for an owner who wants to dispose of his or her interest against the wishes of other co-owners
Feedback / Explanation: A partition suit is a legal avenue for an owner who wants to dispose of his or her interest against the wishes of other co-owners. The suit petitions the court to divide, or partition, the property physically, according to the owner's respective rights and interests

Question: Community property consists of property
A: owned by either spouse at the time of the marriage
B: acquired by either spouse through inheritance during the marriage
D: acquired by either party during the marriage
Feedback / Explanation: Community property law distinguishes real and personal property into categories of separate and community property. Separate property belongs to one spouse; community property belongs to both spouses equally. Community property consists of all property earned or acquired by either party during the marriage

Question: In a community property state, upon the death of the separate property owner, the property
A: passes to the heirs by will or laws of descent
B: automatically passes to the surviving spouse regardless of an existing will
D: will be owned equally by the surviving spouse and heirs
Feedback / Explanation: Upon the death of the separate property owner, the property passes to heirs by will or laws of descent

Question: In an estate in trust, the fee owner is known as the
Feedback / Explanation: In an estate in trust, a fee owner-- the grantor or trustor-- transfers legal title to a fiduciary-- the trustee-- who holds and manages the estate for the benefit of another party, the beneficiary

Question: In an estate in trust, a fee owner transfers legal title to a fiduciary known as the
Feedback / Explanation: In an estate in trust, a fee owner-- the grantor or trustor-- transfers legal title to a fiduciary-- the trustee-- who holds and manages the estate for the benefit of another party, the beneficiary

Question: Which of the following statements is true regarding the provisions of a living trust?
A: A living trust allows the trustor, during his or­­ her lifetime, to convey title to a trustee
B: A living trust allows the trustor to convey title upon the death of the trustor
C: The trustee receives all income and sales proceeds
D: A living trust is the same as a will
Feedback / Explanation: A living trust allows the trustor, during his or­­ her lifetime, to convey title to a trustee for the benefit of a third party. The trustor charges the trustee with all necessary responsibilities for managing the property, protecting its value, and securing whatever income it may produce

Question: Which of the following statements is true regarding the provisions of a land trust?
A: In a land trust, the beneficiary can never be trustor
B: In a land trust, the beneficiary must be the trustee
C: The land trust applies only to real property, not to personal property
D: The land trust applies only to personal property, not to real property
Feedback / Explanation: A land trust allows the trustor to convey the fee estate to the trustee and to name himself or herself the beneficiary. The land trust applies only to real property, not to personal property. The agreement, or deed in trust, grants the beneficiary the right to possess and use the property and to exercise control over the actions of the trustee

Question: The distinguishing features of the land trust are all of the following EXCEPT
C: the beneficiary is not identified on public records
D: the term of the land trust is limited
Feedback / Explanation: beneficiary controls property, beneficiary controls trustee, beneficiary identity not on record, limited term.

Question: Which of the following statements is true regarding one’s liability in a limited partnership?
A: The general partners bear full liability for debts and obligations
B: The general partners bear no liability
C: The limited partners bear full liability
D: All the partners are protected from bearing any personal liability
Feedback / Explanation: All the general partners in a limited partnership bear full liability for debts and obligations. A limited partnership has two or more partners, one or more being general partners and the others limited partners. The general partners run the business and are liable for debts and obligations. The limited partners are liable only to the extent of their investment in the partnership

Question: The unique aspect of the condominium is its _________________ interest in the airspace contained within the outer walls, floors, and ceiling of the building unit
Feedback / Explanation: The unique aspect of the condominium is its fee simple interest in the airspace contained within the outer walls, floors, and ceiling of the building unit. This airspace may include internal walls, which are not essential to the structural support of the building

Question: The common elements in a condominium include all of the following EXCEPT
Feedback / Explanation: Common elements are all portions of the property that are necessary for the existence, operation, and maintenance of the condominium units. Common elements include the land (if not leased); structural components of the building, such as exterior windows, roof, and foundation; physical operating systems supporting all units, such as plumbing, power, communications installations, and central air conditioning; recreational facilities; building and ground areas used non-exclusively, such as stairways, elevators, hallways, and laundry rooms

Question: In a condominium, the unit owners hold an exclusive interest in their individual apartments and co-own common elements with other unit owners as
Feedback / Explanation: The condominium unit can be owned jointly, in severalty, in trust, or in any other manner allowed by state law. Unit owners hold an exclusive interest in their individual apartments and co-own common elements with other unit owners as tenants in common

Question: Condominium properties are created by executing and recording a
A: management agreement and an owner’s association
B: condominium declaration and a master deed
D: legal description and a proprietary lease
Feedback / Explanation: Condominium properties are created by executing and recording a condominium declaration and a master deed

Question: Which of the following statements regarding condominiums is true?
A: The shareholders of a condominium acquire a proprietary lease
B: Each condominium unit is individually assessed and taxed
C: A lender may foreclose on the entire condominium building if a unit owner defaults
D: A condominium unit owner’s interest is personal property
Feedback / Explanation: Condominium units are individually assessed and taxed. The assessment pertains to the value of the exclusive interest in the apartment as well as the unit's pro-rata share of common elements

Question: Which of the following statements regarding cooperatives is true?
A: The shareholders of a cooperative acquire a proprietary lease
B: Each cooperative unit is individually assessed and taxed
C: A lender may foreclose on a single unit if the owner of that unit defaults
D: A cooperative unit owner’s interest is real property
Feedback / Explanation: In a cooperative, or co-op, one owns shares in a non-profit corporation or cooperative association, which in turn acquires and owns an apartment building as its principal asset. Along with this stock, the shareholder acquires a proprietary lease to occupy one of the apartment units

Question: The corporate entity of the cooperative association is the only party in the cooperative with
D: an ownership interest with individual units
Feedback / Explanation: The corporate entity of the cooperative association is the only party in the cooperative with a real property interest. The association's interest is an undivided interest in the entire property. There is no ownership interest in individual units, as with a condominium

Question: Which type of ownership is described as a fee or leasehold interest in a property whose owners or tenants agree to use the property on a periodic, non-overlapping basis?
Feedback / Explanation: Time-share ownership is a fee or leasehold interest in a property whose owners or tenants agree to use the property on a periodic, non-overlapping basis. This type of ownership commonly concerns vacation and resort properties

Question: A freehold time-share is also known as
Feedback / Explanation: In a freehold time-share, or interval ownership estate, tenants in common own undivided interests in the property. Expense prorations and rules governing interval usage are established by separate agreement when the estate is acquired

Question: Which of the following terms is defined “as personal and real property ownership created to take effect during one's lifetime or after one's lifetime”?
Feedback / Explanation: The definition of living trust is personal and real property ownership created to take effect during one's lifetime (living trust) or after one's lifetime (testamentary)

Question: Which of the following terms is defined as “equal, undivided interest jointly owned by husband and wife”?
Feedback / Explanation: The definition of tenancy by the entirety is equal, undivided interest jointly owned by husband and wife

Question: Which of the following terms is defined as “ownership of shares in owning corporation, plus proprietary lease in a unit; corporation has sole, undivided ownership”?
Feedback / Explanation: The definition of cooperatives is ownership of shares in owning a corporation, plus proprietary lease in a unit; corporation has sole, undivided ownership living trust allows the trustor, during his or­­ her lifetime, to convey title to a trustee for the benefit of a third party

Question: The co-op interest is transferred by
A: recording the deed in county records
B: delivering a deed to the buyer
C: assigning both the stock certificates and lease to the buyer
D: signing a living trust and transferring interest to the buyer
Feedback / Explanation: The co-op interest is transferred by assigning both the stock certificates and lease to the buyer

Question: The proprietor-tenant in a cooperative is responsible for the
A: unit's pro-rata share of the corporation's expenses
Feedback / Explanation: The co-op lease is called a proprietary lease because the tenant is an owner (proprietor) of the corporation that owns the property. The lease has no stated or fixed rent. Instead, the proprietor-tenant is responsible for the unit's pro-rata share of the corporation's expenses in supporting the cooperative. Unit owners pay monthly assessments

Question: Which of the following is a feature of a corporation?
C: The corporation may be own real estate in severalty
D: The corporation may own real estate by the entirety
Feedback / Explanation: A corporation is a legal entity owned by stockholders.  An elected board of directors oversees the business. Officers and managers conduct day-to-day activities. Officers and directors may be held fully liable for the corporation’s actions, while shareholders are liable only to the extent of the value of their shares. Corporations, like individuals, may own real estate in severalty or as tenants in common

Question: Which of the following is true of a tenancy in common?
B: The tenants share an indivisible interest
C: The tenants must acquire their interests at the same time
D: The tenants own equal shares of the estate
Feedback / Explanation: The tenants share an indivisible interest of the common elements

Question: A tenant in common can
A: sell, encumber or transfer his or her interest to an outside party without the consent of the other tenants in common
B: use his or her interest in the estate to encumber the entire estate
C: sell, encumber or transfer his or her interest only to the other tenants in common
D: sell, encumber or transfer his or her interest only with the consent of all the other tenants in common
Feedback / Explanation: All tenants in common have distinct and separable ownership of their respective interests. Co-tenants may sell, encumber, or transfer their interests without obstruction or consent from the other owners

Question: When a tenant in common dies, what happens to the tenant's interest in the estate?
A: It is divided equally among the surviving tenants in common
B: The surviving tenants must buy the interest from the deceased tenant's heirs or sell their interests to the heirs
D: It passes by probate to the deceased tenant's heirs
Feedback / Explanation: A deceased co-tenant's estate passes by probate to the decedent's heirs and devisees rather than to the other tenants in common. Any number of heirs can share in the ownership of the willed tenancy

Question: Which of the following is true of a joint tenancy?
A: The tenants can determine the size of the share owned by each tenant
B: The size of the tenant's shares is determined by the amount of equity each has invested in the property
C: The tenants have an equal and indivisible ownership interest
D: There can be no more than two co-owners, and each has a fifty percent interest
Feedback / Explanation: In a joint tenancy, two or more persons collectively own a property as if they were a single person. Rights and interests are indivisible and equal: each has a shared interest in the whole property which cannot be divided up. Joint tenants may only convey their interests to outside parties as tenant-in-common interests. One can not convey a joint tenant interest

Question: In contrast to a tenancy in common, in a joint tenancy
A: there is a single title to the property
B: there are as many titles to the property as there are co-owners
C: title is held by a corporate entity
D: co-owners who are married hold separate titles
Feedback / Explanation: Whereas tenants in common hold separate title to their individual interests, joint tenants together hold a single title to the property. This is referred to as unity of ownership

Question: If a joint tenant sells his or her interest to an outside party,
A: the new owner becomes a joint tenant
B: the new owner is a tenant in common
C: the joint tenancy terminates and all owners become tenants in common
D: the joint tenancy terminates and the owners must create a new joint tenancy to include the new owner
Feedback / Explanation: A joint tenant may transfer his or her interest in the property to an outside party, but only as a tenancy in common interest. Whoever acquires the interest co-owns the property as a tenant in common with the other joint tenants

Question: When a joint tenant dies, what happens to the tenant's interest in the estate?
A: It passes to the decedent's heirs, who become joint tenants
B: It passes as a tenancy in common to the decedent's heirs
C: The joint tenancy terminates and becomes a tenancy in common with the decedent's heirs and the surviving tenants as co-owners
D: It passes to the surviving joint tenants
Feedback / Explanation: n most states, joint tenants enjoy rights of survivorship: if a joint tenant dies, all interests and rights pass to the surviving joint tenants free from any claims of creditors or heirs. When only one joint tenant survives, the survivor's interest becomes an estate in severalty, and the joint tenancy is terminated

Question: The most common form of co-ownership when the owners are not married is
Feedback / Explanation: The tenancy in common, also known as the estate in common, is the most common form of co-ownership when the owners are not married

Question: If a co-owner in a joint tenancy or tenancy in common wants to dispose of his or her interest against the wishes of the other co-owners, the co-owner can
A: sue in court for a termination of the tenancy and partition of the property
B: deed the property to himself or herself as a tenancy in severalty
C: create a trust with himself or herself as sole beneficiary
D: file a foreclosure suit to force the other owners to sell their interests
Feedback / Explanation: A partition suit is a legal avenue for an owner who wants to dispose of his or her interest against the wishes of other co-owners. The suit petitions the court to divide, or partition, the property physically, according to the owner's respective rights and interests. If this is not reasonably feasible, the court may order the property sold, whereupon the interests are liquidated and distributed proportionately

Question: Two individuals can own a house as tenants by the entireties if
A: they so elect at the time of acquiring title
Feedback / Explanation: Tenancy by the entireties is a form of ownership traditionally reserved for husband and wife

Question: One of the benefits offered by ownership in a tenancy by the entireties is
A: the estate is subject to foreclosure only for jointly incurred debts
B: the estate may be terminated only by mutual agreement
C: the estate is not subject to foreclosure
D: a co-owner may transfer his or her fractional interest without the consent of other co-owners
Feedback / Explanation: No foreclosure for individual debts. The estate is subject to foreclosure only for jointly incurred debts

Question: When an estate is held in a trust, who holds legal title?
Feedback / Explanation: In an estate in trust, a fee owner-- the grantor or trustor-- transfers legal title to a fiduciary-- the trustee-- who holds and manages the estate for the benefit of another party, the beneficiary

Question: When real property is held in a land trust, who controls the property?
D: The property manager appointed by the trustee
Feedback / Explanation: Beneficiary controls property - this includes occupancy and control of rents and sale proceeds

Question: A condominium owner can
A: sell or mortgage the condominium unit without hindrance from individual owners of neighboring units
B: sell the interest in the physical unit separately from the interest in the common elements
C: exclude non-owners from the unit owner's pro rata portion of the common elements
D: exclusively possess and use those portions of the common areas structurally or functionally necessary for the operation of the unit
Feedback / Explanation: Condominium units can be individually sold, mortgaged, or otherwise encumbered without interference from other unit owners

Question: A condominium owner's assessments for maintenance and operation are based on
A: the unit's pro rata share of floor space
B: the unit's pro rata share of the property value as defined in the declaration
C: the number of shares the owner purchased in the condominium association
D: the assessed value of the condominium unit
Feedback / Explanation: Condominium units are individually assessed and taxed. The assessment pertains to the value of the exclusive interest in the apartment as well as the unit's pro rata share of common elements

Question: The owner of a cooperative owns
A: shares in a corporation or association and a proprietary lease in a physical unit
B: a fee simple interest in a physical unit plus a tenancy in common in common elements
C: a tenancy in common in a physical unit and the common areas
D: a ground lease in the physical unit's pro rata share of land and a proprietary lease in the unit
Feedback / Explanation: In a cooperative, or co-op, one owns shares in a non-profit corporation or cooperative association, which in turn acquires and owns an apartment building as its principal asset. Along with this stock, the shareholder acquires a proprietary lease to occupy one of the apartment units

Question: In a cooperative, real property is owned only by
B: the individual unit owners and the cooperative association
D: the corporate entity of the cooperative association
Feedback / Explanation: The corporate entity of the cooperative association is the only party in the cooperative with a real property interest. The association's interest is an undivided interest in the entire property. There is no ownership interest in individual units, as with a condominium

Question: In a time-share freehold, owners acquire
A: undivided interests as tenants in common
B: a tenancy from period-to-period for a portion of a year
C: a pro rata share of a leased fee
D: a tenancy in severalty for a portion of a year
Feedback / Explanation: In a freehold time-share, or interval ownership estate, tenants in common own undivided interests in the property. Expense prorations and rules governing interval usage are established by separate agreement when the estate is acquired

Question: What is the principal purpose underlying legal descriptions of real property?
A: To create a consistent, unchanging standard for locating the property
B: To eliminate all possible boundary disputes
D: To eliminate cumbersome metes and bounds descriptions
Feedback / Explanation: A legal description of real property is one which accurately locates and identifies the boundaries of the subject parcel to a degree acceptable by courts of law in the state where the property is located. Legal description provides accuracy and consistency over time

Question: Which of the following is a distinctive feature of metes and bounds descriptions?
A: They use meridians and base lines
B: They identify an enclosed area, beginning and ending at the same point
C: They use lot and block numbers.as the street address
D: They incorporate elevation into the descriptions
Feedback / Explanation: A metes and bounds description always identifies an enclosed area by starting at an origination point, called "point of beginning," or POB, and returning to the POB at the end of the description

Question: A certain legal description contains the phrase "...southeasterly along Happ Road to the stone landmark..." What kind of description is this?
A: Plat survey plat of survey is
Feedback / Explanation: A metes and bounds description identities the point of beginning and describes the distance and direction from that point to the first monument, and thence to subsequent monuments and back to the beginning to define the property's enclosed perimeter

Question: The abbreviation POB stands for
Feedback / Explanation: POB stands for "point of beginning."

Question: What are the approximate dimensions of a township in the rectangular survey system?
Feedback / Explanation: A township is a square with six miles on each side. Its area is 36 square miles

Question: The area running north and south between meridians is a
Feedback / Explanation: The north-south area between consecutive meridians is called a range

Question: The area running east and west between base lines is a
Feedback / Explanation: The east-west area between two parallels is called a tier, or a township strip

Question: How many sections are there in a township?
Feedback / Explanation: The rectangular survey system divides a township into thirty-six sections

Question: A section contains how many acres?
Feedback / Explanation: Each side of a section is one mile in length. Thus the area of a section is one square mile, or 640 acres

Question: How many acres are there in the S 1/2 of the NW ¼ of Section 3?
Feedback / Explanation: Each section is 640 acres. Starting from right to left, the northwest quarter section is 160 acres (640 ÷ 4). The southern 1/2 of the 160 acres is 80 acres (160 ÷ 2)

Question: If a parcel does not have a lot and block number and is too irregular to be described as a fraction of a section, the legal description
B: will include a metes and bounds description
C: will use an estimate of the sectional fraction
D: will create a special reference number
Feedback / Explanation: For an irregular shape, such as a triangle, the full description has to include a metes and bounds or lot and block description

Question: The legal description of a parcel in a subdivision that has been recorded with lot and block numbers on a plat of survey is
A: the lot and block number, with section, township and meridian references
Feedback / Explanation: The subdivision plat map is recorded in the county where the subdivision is located. The recorded lot and block numbers of a subdivision parcel, along with its section, township and meridian reference, become the property's legal description

Question: A datum is a reference point used for legal descriptions of
C: properties located above or below the earth's surface
Feedback / Explanation: To describe property located above or below the earth's surface, a surveyor must know the property's elevation. Standard elevation reference points, called "datums," have been established throughout the country

Question: A description of a parcel of property which accurately locates and identifies the boundaries of the subject parcel to a degree acceptable by courts of law is known as
Feedback / Explanation: A legal description of real property is one which accurately locates and identifies the boundaries of the subject parcel to a degree acceptable by courts of law in the state where the property is located

Question: The general criterion for a legal description is that
A: it provides sufficient data for the average person to locate the parcel
B: it provides sufficient data to convey title
D: it provides sufficient data for a surveyor to locate the parcel
Feedback / Explanation: The general criterion for a legal description is that it alone provides sufficient data for a surveyor to locate the parcel. A legal description identifies the property as unique and distinct from all other properties

Question: Methods employed in legal descriptions facilitate all the following EXCEPT
Feedback / Explanation: Legal descriptions facilitate transfers of ownership, prevent boundary disputes and assist in resolving problems with encroachments and chain of title

Question: Which of the following activities requires a legal description?
A: Creating a valid deed of conveyance
Feedback / Explanation: A legal description is required for: public recording, creating a valid deed of conveyance or lease, completing mortgage documents, executing and recording other legal documents

Question: A legal description provides a basis for
A: court rulings on nonconforming use and variances
B: court rulings on encroachments and easements
Feedback / Explanation: A legal description provides a basis for court rulings on encroachments and easements

Question: Which of the following legal description method is generally used in the East Coast states?
Feedback / Explanation: Since the metes and bounds method preceded the inception of the rectangular survey system, the older East Coast states generally employ metes and bounds descriptions. States in the Midwest and West predominantly use the rectangular survey system. Some states combine methods

Question: Which of the following statements is true regarding the metes and bounds system?
A: A metes and bounds description must return to the point of beginning (POB)
B: The term metes refer to monuments and landmarks
C: The surveyor is not allowed to use artificial markers as stakes
D: The metes and bounds method is predominantly used in the Midwest
Feedback / Explanation: A metes and bounds description identifies the boundaries of a parcel of real estate using reference points, distances, and angles. The description always identifies an enclosed area by starting at an origination point, called point of beginning, or POB, and returning to the POB at the end of the description. A metes and bounds description must return to the POB in order to be valid

Question: The north-south, longitudinal lines on the survey grid are called
Feedback / Explanation: The north-south, longitudinal lines on the survey grid are meridians. The principal meridian is the single designated meridian for identifying townships in the principal meridian's geographical "jurisdiction." There are 37 principal meridians in the national survey

Question: The southeast corner of every township is always identified as which section?
Feedback / Explanation: The sections in a township are numbered sequentially starting with Section 1 in the northeast corner, proceeding east to west across the top row, continuing from west to east across the next lower row, and so on, alternately, ending with Section 36 in the southeast corner

Question: How many acres are contained in the SW1/4 of the SW1/2 of Section 16?
Feedback / Explanation: 640 divided by 4 divided by 2 = 80

Question: How many acres are contained in the SW1/4 and the SW1/2 of Section 16?
The recorded plat method is also called the
Feedback / Explanation: The recorded plat method, also called the lot and block system is used to describe properties in residential, commercial, and industrial subdivisions

Question: Standard elevation reference points, called ___________, have been established throughout the country
Feedback / Explanation: To describe property located above or below the earth's surface, such as the air rights of a condominium, a surveyor must know the property's elevation. Standard elevation reference points, called datums, have been established throughout the country

Question: Surveyors have identified local elevation markers, called ____________, to provide reference elevations for nearby properties
Feedback / Explanation: In many cases, it is impractical for a surveyor to rely on a single datum for an entire surveying area. To simplify matters, surveyors have identified local elevation markers, called benchmarks, to provide reference elevations for nearby properties. Once a benchmark is registered, it provides a valid reference point for surveying other elevations in the immediate area

Question: Which of the following is used in surveyed subdivisions?
Feedback / Explanation: The recorded plat method, or lot and block system is used in surveyed subdivisions

Question: A legal description of a property is one which
A: accurately locates and identifies the boundaries of the property and distinguishes it from all other properties
B: accurately identifies the boundaries of the property and describes the location and dimensions of any improvements on the property
C: is accepted by a licensed surveyor as suitable for inclusion in a survey of the property
D: written by an attorney licensed to practice real estate law in the state in which the property is located
Feedback / Explanation: A legal description of real property is one which accurately locates and identifies the boundaries of the subject parcel to a degree acceptable by courts of law in the state where the property is located. The general criterion for a legal description is that it alone provides sufficient data for a surveyor to locate the parcel. A legal description identifies the property as unique and distinct from all other properties

Question: What method of legal description was the first one used in America?
Feedback / Explanation: A metes and bounds description identifies the point of beginning and describes the distance and direction from that point to the first monument, and thence to subsequent monuments and back to the beginning to define the property's enclosed perimeter

Question: A certain property has the following general features: Which of the following statements would provide a (partial) metes and bounds description of the property?
A: Commencing at A, thence south 0 degrees east to B, thence north 45 degrees west to C, thence north 0 degrees west to D, thence north 90 degrees east to the point of beginning
B: Commencing at A, thence 90 degrees south to B, thence west 45 degrees north to C,  then east to the point of beginning
C: Commencing at A, thence south 90 degrees west to B, thence north 45 degrees west to C, thence north 0 degrees east to D, thence 90 degrees east to the point of beginning
D: Commencing at A, thence south 0 degrees east to B, thence west 45 degrees north to C, thence north 0 degrees east to D, thence east 0 degrees north to the point of beginning
Feedback / Explanation: The metes and bounds descriptions begin and end at the same point.  Only option A will get you back to the POB

Question: The fundamental components of the metes and bounds system are
B: boundaries, distances, and a base line
Feedback / Explanation: A metes and bounds description identifies the boundaries of a parcel of real estate using reference points, distances, and angles. The description always identifies an enclosed area by starting at an origination point, called point of beginning, or POB, and returning to the POB at the end of the description

Question: A legal description in the rectangular survey system will also include a metes and bounds or lot and block description if the property
B: is more than six miles from a standard parallel
Feedback / Explanation: For an irregular shape, such as a triangle, the full description has to include a metes and bounds or lot and block description

Question: Which of the following best describes a meridian?
A: An east-west line that corresponds to a latitude on a rectangular survey grid
B: An east-west latitudinal  line 6 miles in length used to describe a township’s north or south border
C: A north-south longitudinal line on a rectangular survey grid
D: A north-south longitudinal line 6 miles in length used to describe a township’s east or west border
Feedback / Explanation: The north-south, longitudinal lines on a rectangular survey grid are meridians

Question: What is the purpose of a principal meridian and base parallel?
A: Used to divide townships into sections
C: To establish the distance between all meridians and parallels
D: To differentiate a tier from a range
Feedback / Explanation: The principal meridian is the single designated meridian for identifying townships in the principal meridian's geographical "jurisdiction." The base parallel or base line is the designated line for identifying townships

Question: How far apart are parallels?
Feedback / Explanation: Parallels are for identifying townships. Townships are six miles by six miles in size, making parallels six miles apart

Question: Which of the following is true?
A: A township is the area enclosed by principal meridians and base parallels
B: A township’s sections are six miles apart
C: There are 36 townships in a section
D: There are 36 sections in a township
Feedback / Explanation: A township is a square with six miles on each side. Its area is 36 square miles. Each section within a township is one square mile, making 36 sections in a township

Question: A range is defined by
C: the distance between a principal meridian and a base parallel
Feedback / Explanation: The "vertical" area between consecutive meridians is called a range. A range is identified by its relationship to the principal meridian. All ranges are six miles wide

Question: A tier is defined by
Feedback / Explanation: The "horizontal" area between two parallels is called a tier, or a township strip. A tier is identified by its relationship to the base parallel. All tiers are six miles wide

Question: What are the dimensions of a township?
Feedback / Explanation: A township is the area enclosed by the intersection of two consecutive meridians and two consecutive parallels. Since the parallels and meridians are six miles apart, a township is a square with six miles on each side. Its area is therefore 36 square miles

Question: Sections of a township are numbered in which of the following directions?
A: From the northeast corner toward the west and on to the southeast corner, alternating direction in each row
B: From the northwest corner toward the east and on to the southwest corner, alternating direction in each row
C: North to south from left to right in each row, starting in the northwest corner
D: Clockwise in a spiral beginning in the northeast corner
Feedback / Explanation: The sections in a township are numbered sequentially starting with Section 1 in the northeast corner, proceeding east to west across the top row, continuing from west to east across the next lower row, and so on, alternately, ending with Section 36 in the southeast corner

Question: What portion of a section is ten acres?
Feedback / Explanation: The size in acres of a subsection of a township is a fraction of 640 acres, since there are 640 acres in a section. Thus ten acres is 10/640, or 1/64th of a section

Question: A tract of land is 1/32nd of a section.  How many acres does this tract consist of?
Feedback / Explanation: A section is 640 acres. 640 ÷ 32 = 20 acres

Question: The lot and block system of description is used for
B: any property in an incorporated area
Feedback / Explanation: The recorded plat method, also called the lot and block system, is used to describe properties in residential, commercial, and industrial subdivisions. Under this system, tracts of land are subdivided into lots. The entire group of lots comprises the subdivision. In a large subdivision, lots may be grouped together into blocks for ease of reference
